name: tailwind
context: Tailwind CSS utility-first framework — classes, config, responsive design, dark mode, plugins, and v4 migration
info: Tailwind CSS is a utility-first CSS framework that lets agents and developers build UIs by composing single-purpose utility classes directly in markup rather than writing custom CSS. It scans project files for class names and generates only the CSS actually used, producing tiny optimized stylesheets. Tailwind is the dominant styling approach in modern frontend stacks — Next.js, Vite, SvelteKit, Nuxt, and most React projects ship with it. Agents encounter Tailwind in virtually every frontend task: adding components, fixing layouts, implementing responsive designs, toggling dark mode, and configuring build pipelines. This ecosystem covers utility classes ([[tailwind/classes]]), project configuration ([[tailwind/config]]), responsive breakpoints and container queries ([[tailwind/responsive]]), dark mode strategies ([[tailwind/dark-mode]]), the plugin system ([[tailwind/plugins]]), and the v4 rewrite with CSS-first configuration ([[tailwind/v4]]).
body:
# Tailwind CSS Tailwind CSS is a utility-first CSS framework. Instead of writing custom CSS, you compose designs from small, single-purpose utility classes applied directly in HTML or JSX. ## Core Concept ```html
ChitChat

You have a new message!

``` Tailwind scans your source files, detects which classes you use, and generates a minimal CSS file containing only those styles. ## Ecosystem Map | Node | Type | Purpose | |------|------|---------| | [[tailwind/classes]] | skill | Apply utility classes — layout, spacing, typography, colors, effects | | [[tailwind/config]] | skill | Set up and customize Tailwind — install, theme, plugins, content detection | | [[tailwind/responsive]] | context | Breakpoints, mobile-first design, container queries | | [[tailwind/dark-mode]] | context | Dark mode strategies — media, class, data-attribute | | [[tailwind/plugins]] | context | Plugin API, official plugins, custom utilities and variants | | [[tailwind/v4]] | context | v4 rewrite — CSS-first config, @theme, performance, migration from v3 | ## Installation (v4) ```bash # Vite (recommended) npm install tailwindcss @tailwindcss/vite # PostCSS npm install tailwindcss @tailwindcss/postcss ``` ```css /* app.css — single import, no config file needed */ @import "tailwindcss"; ``` ## Why Tailwind - **No naming**: No time spent inventing class names like `.card-wrapper-inner` - **Scoped changes**: Changing a utility only affects the element it's on - **Small output**: CSS doesn't grow linearly with features — unused classes are never generated - **Design system**: Constrained spacing, color, and typography scales prevent magic numbers - **State variants**: `hover:`, `focus:`, `disabled:`, `group-hover:` built in - **Responsive**: `sm:`, `md:`, `lg:`, `xl:`, `2xl:` breakpoint prefixes - **Dark mode**: `dark:` prefix for theming

sections.title: Utility-first approach
sections.body: Tailwind provides single-purpose utility classes: `flex`, `p-6`, `rounded-xl`, `bg-white`, `shadow-lg`, `text-xl`, `font-medium`. You compose them in markup instead of writing CSS rules. Each utility maps to one CSS property-value pair. Variants like `hover:`, `sm:`, `dark:` modify when the utility applies. This approach eliminates the indirection of semantic class names and makes styles visible at the point of use.

sections.title: How Tailwind generates CSS
sections.body: Tailwind scans all source files (HTML, JSX, TSX, Vue, Svelte, etc.) for class names. It extracts every candidate, generates the corresponding CSS rules, and writes a single optimized stylesheet. In v4, content detection is automatic — Tailwind respects `.gitignore` and skips binary files. No `content` array in a config file is needed. Use `@source` to add paths not auto-detected: `@source "../node_modules/@my-company/ui-lib";`

sections.title: Arbitrary values and CSS variables
sections.body: For one-off values outside the theme, use square bracket syntax: `bg-[#316ff6]`, `w-[calc(100%-2rem)]`, `grid-cols-[24rem_1fr]`. Set CSS variables inline: `[--gutter:1rem] lg:[--gutter:2rem]`. Reference theme variables in arbitrary values: `rounded-[calc(var(--radius-xl)-1px)]`. Arbitrary values let you stay in the utility workflow without adding every value to the theme.

sections.title: Stacking variants
sections.body: Variants compose by stacking prefixes left to right: `dark:lg:hover:bg-indigo-600` applies the background in dark mode, at lg breakpoint, on hover. Order doesn't matter semantically but conventionally goes: responsive > state > dark. Group variants target children based on parent state: `group-hover:underline`. Peer variants target siblings: `peer-invalid:text-red-500`.

links.context: Apply utility classes for layout, spacing, typography, colors

related.note: v4 fundamentally changes how Tailwind is configured — CSS-first instead of JS
